coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots with aggregation induced quenching effect and solvatochromism for the detection of H2O in organic solvents
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xiaoyu
Teng, Xiuming
Sun, Xiaobo
Pan, Wei
Wang, Jinping
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a type of carbon dots (CDs) which can be well dispersed in organic solvents but precipitated in neutral aqueous solution was synthesized by using one-step hydrothermal heating treatment of o-phenylenediamine in H2SO4 solution. Although the emissions of the CDs in different organic solvents are excitation independent, solvatochromism is observed for the CDs since the emission wavelength is red shifted with an increase in the polarity of solvents. The optical properties suggest that the emission of the CDs is controlled by molecular state. According to aggregation induced quenching effect and solvatochromism caused by an increase in the solvent polarity with the content of H2O increasing, detection methods of H2O in the organic solvents were developed by using the CDs as a probe. The spiked H2O in acetone and methanol could be recovered in the range of 98.2% to 101.7%, which indicates that the as-proposed method has a high potential for the determination of H2O in organic solvents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Solvatochromism
Determination
Organic solvents
H2O

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The photograph of the CDs in (A) aprotic solvents (Ethyl acetate, Acetone, Acetonitrile, DMF, DMSO) and (B) protic solvents (Ethanol, Methanol, EG) taken under day light and 365 nm UV light.

The TEM images of the CDs in solvents (A) Ethyl acetate, (B) Acetonitrile, (C) DMF, (D) DMSO, (E) Ethanol, (F) Methanol, (G) EG. (Insets: the particle size distributions of the CDs in the corresponding solvents); (H) The FT-IR spectrum, and (I) a) XRD and b) Raman spectra of the solid powder of the CDs.
The normalized fluorescence spectra in (A) aprotic solvents and (B) protic solvents, and the normalized absorption spectra in (C) aprotic solvents and (D) protic solvents.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs (0.005 mg mL−1) at different water content in methanol under excitation wavelength at 500 nm. (B) A linear relationship of fluorescence intensity versus water content in methanol. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs (0.01 mg mL−1) at different water content in acetone under excitation wavelength at 500 nm. (D) A linear relationship of emission peak wavelength versus water content in acetone.
Schematic illustration for the preparation of the CDs with aggregation induced quenching effect and solvatochromism, and the application in the detection of H2O in organic solvents.
Results for the detection of water content in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Carbon dots with aggregation induced quenching effect and solvatochromism for the detection of H2O in organic solvents

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a type of carbon dots (CDs) which can be well dispersed in organic solvents but precipitated in neutral aqueous solution was synthesized by using one-step hydrothermal heating treatment of o-phenylenediamine in H2SO4 solution. Although the emissions of the CDs in different organic solvents are excitation independent, solvatochromism is observed for the CDs since the emission wavelength is red shifted with an increase in the polarity of solvents. The optical properties suggest that the emission of the CDs is controlled by molecular state. According to aggregation induced quenching effect and solvatochromism caused by an increase in the solvent polarity with the content of H2O increasing, detection methods of H2O in the organic solvents were developed by using the CDs as a probe. The spiked H2O in acetone and methanol could be recovered in the range of 98.2% to 101.7%, which indicates that the as-proposed method has a high potential for the determination of H2O in organic solvents.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are considered as one of the most intensively explored optical nanomaterials in the last few years. They have exhibited excellent properties including stable photoluminescence, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, robust chemical inertness and ease of preparation [1–3]. Based on these properties, CDs have been widely applied in fluorescent probes [2,4], bioimaging [5,6], light-emitting devices [7,8] and drug delivery [9]. However, extensive application of CDs may suffer from short-wavelength emissions that are predominant for most of the current CDs. Although many strategies, including doping [10,11], surface tailoring [12] and so on, have been explored to synthesize CDs with long-wavelength (red, infrared) emission, most of them are not “real” red or infrared. In fact, they are of excitation-dependent multicolor emissions controlled by surface states, in which, long-wavelength ones are generated under excitation only at long wavelengths and their intensities are generally weak [13]. Moreover, the long-wavelength emissions controlled by surface states are usually not pure because short-wavelength emissions may be generated if long-wavelength excitation is mixed with short-wavelength light.

On the other hand, molecular state controlled emission is commonly excitation-independent, that is, the emission wavelength is fixed with excitation wavelength changing [14,15]. Therefore, molecular state controlled emission is relatively pure even though excitation wavelength moves from long to short ones. More importantly, solvent-dependence is a characteristic of the molecular state controlled emission of CDs [16,17]. Hence, using organic solvents with different polarities to tune the molecular state controlled emission is a considerably effective approach to obtaining relative pure multicolor fluorescence lights.

In addition, water is the common impurity in organic solvents that can be harmful to organic synthesis and industrial processes [18,19]. In case of moisture-sensitive chemical reactions, for example, organometallic chemistry, trace amount of water may inhibit the efficiency, yield, selectivity, and catalyst performance of the reactions [20,21]. Hence, sensitive and selective methods for the detection of water in organic solvents are highly desired. Although various kinds of traditional methods have been applied for the detection of water, toxicity, high cost, and demands for specific instruments performed by well trained professionals may hinder their extensive applications [20,22,23]. The unique optical properties combined with effective cost, low toxicity and environmental friendliness make CDs to be potential probes for the detection of water in organic solvents. Thereby, aggregation induced quenching (AIQ) effect caused by different dispersity of CDs in water and organic solvents, as well as polarity induced solvatochromism upon addition of water can be the response mechanisms for the detection water in organic solvents.

In this work, a type of CDs which can be well dispersed in organic solvents but precipitated in neutral aqueous solution was synthesized by using one-step hydrothermal heating treatment of o-phenylenediamine in H2SO4 solution. The emissions of the CDs in different organic solvents are excitation independent, and solvatochromism is observed for the CDs since the emission wavelength is red shifted with an increase in the polarity of solvents. The optical properties suggest that the emission of the CDs is controlled by molecular state. According to AIQ effect and solvatochromism caused by an increase in the content of H2O, detection methods for H2O in organic solvents were developed by using the CDs as a probe (Scheme1 ).

H2SO4, NaOH, o-phenylenediamine, citric acid, methanol, acetone, ethanol, acetonitrile, N, N - dimethylformamide (DMF), ethylene glycol (EG) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were used without any further purification. The ultrapure water used throughout all the experiments was purified through Water Purifier Nanopure water system (18.3 MΩ.cm).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken in a Hitachi H-7700 operating at 80 kV. UV–vis absorption spectra were performed using a U-3900 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded using a D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS, German) with Cu-Kα radiation (40 kV, 40 mA, λ = 1.5418 Å) at a scanning rate of 1° min−1 in the range from 10° to 80°. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were taken on a FT-IR200 spectrometer (Thermo, America). Fluorescent spectra were collected by a fluorescence spectrometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan). Absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) were measured by a FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, England).

0.2 g o-phenylenediamine and 20 mL H2SO4 (0.75 M) were added into a 50 mL Teflon-equipped stainless steel autoclave, respectively. Furthermore, the mixture was hydrothermally treated for 8 h at 180 ℃ in a drying oven. After it was allowed to cool to room temperature, the product was neutralized to pH = 7 using NaOH (10 M), and black solids were precipitated. The precipitate was collected by allowing the solution to pass through a 0.22 μm micron filter so as to remove unreacted raw material and a majority of salt. Finally, the product was dried in a vacuum at 60 ℃ for further use. The organic solutions of CDs were obtained by dispersing the solid powder of the CDs in organic solvents, followed by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 10 min.

The CDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine in H2SO4 solution at 180 ℃ for 8 h. Then the CDs were precipitated using NaOH to neutralize the product solution to pH 7.0, followed by a simple purification process, i.e. ultrapure water washing for several times. After dried in a vacuum at 60 ℃, the final solid product was obtained. Although the CDs were presented as precipitate in neutral aqueous solution, interestingly, they can be well dispersed in a series of organic solvents, such as DMSO, EG, ethanol, methanol, DMF, acetonitrile, acetone, and ethyl acetate (Fig. 1 ). The CDs in the organic solvents emitted different color fluorescence under UV light, which can be easily discriminated to the naked eyes (Fig. 1).

The morphologies of the CDs in different solvents were characterized by TEM, and the size distribution of the CDs in the individual organic solvent was measured by counting 100 dots in the corresponding TEM image. As can be seen from Fig. 2 A-D, the morphologies of the CDs are all near spherical, the average sizes of the CDs are 2.1 ± 0.3, 2.3 ± 0.3, 2.7 ± 0.3 and 4.6 ± 0.5 nm in ethyl acetate, acetonitrile, DMF and DMSO, respectively. Apparently, with an increase in the polarity of aprotic solvent, the average size of the CDs gradually increases, suggesting that there was interaction between the polarity of aprotic solvent and the size distribution of the CDs. And the average sizes of the CDs are 2.2 ± 0.3, 7.5 ± 0.9 and 1.9 ± 0.3 nm in protic solvents, respectively (Fig. 2E-G). There are no obvious characteristic change rules of the particle size distribution, the reason may be that there were H bonds formed between the CDs and the protic solvents, and the H bonds and the polarity of solvent together caused the irregular change of the particle size.

FT-IR spectrum was collected to explore the functional groups on the surface of the solid CDs. As shown in Fig. 2H, the broad peaks at 3664 and 3433 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H, respectively. The two sharp absorption peaks at 2976, 2897 cm−1 are corresponding to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of C-H. And the peak at 1832 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of C = O. The absorption peaks at 1382 and 1063 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibration of C = C/C-N and C-O-C, respectively. The crystalline nature of the CDs was characterized by XRD and is shown in Fig. 2I(a). A broad peak in the XRD pattern of the CDs at 26.2° is observed with d = 0.34 nm, which is very consistent with the (002) facet of graphite-like carbon based materials [24].

The Raman spectrum of the CDs shows two main peaks including the D band (sp3 defect) at 1367 cm−1 and the G band (sp2 carbon) at 1534 cm−1 (Fig. 2I(b)). The D band is attributed to the vibration of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of disordered graphite. The G band corresponds to the E2g mode of graphite and is related to the vibration of sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice [25]. The ratio of IG/ID for the CDs is ∼1.12, indicating a high crystallinity of the CDs.

The optical properties of the CDs in the organic solvents were investigated by collecting their fluorescence and absorption spectra. From Fig. S1, the spectra of the CDs in each organic solvent display an excitation-independent property, that is, the peak wavelengths do not move with excitation wavelength changing. Apparently, the fluorescence emissions of the CDs depend on the properties of the organic solvents, which is called solvent-dependent emission behavior. As can be seen from Fig. 3 A and S2A, with an increase in the polarity of aprotic solvents, the emission peaks of the CDs are gradually red-shifted from 569 to 604 nm, suggesting that there was interaction between the polarity and the emission of the CDs. For protic solvents, the maximum emission wavelengths are found at 606, 607 and 612 nm for the CDs in ethanol, methanol and EG, respectively (Fig. 3B and S2B). Obviously, the peak emission position of the CDs in ethanol is almost identical to that in methanol. And only a 6 nm shift occurs even though the polarity of EG is much higher than ethanol. Therefore, the shift of the emission wavelength of the CDs in protic solvents is also dependent on the solvent polarity, but the emission polarity dependence is less than that in aprotic solvents. It can be deduced that there were H bonds formed between the CDs and the protic solvents, and the effect of H bonds is contrary to that of polarity of solvent on the emission of CDs.

The solvent-dependent optical properties of the CDs can be effectively described using Reichardt polarity parameter
E
T
N
, a normalized parameter taking account of the overall solvation capacity of solvents, including non-specific interactions (e.g. dipole–dipole) and specific interactions (e.g. hydrogen bonding) [26]. The emission peaks of the CDs are gradually red-shifted (Fig. S3A) and the PLQY of the CDs gradually decreases (Fig. S3B) with
E
T
N
of solvent increasing (except acetonitrile). The increase in the Stokes shift usually results in a sharp drop in the absolute PLQY [27]. But, the extremely low absolute PLQY of the CDs in water is probably due to its relatively poor solubility (Table S1).

The effect of concentration on the emission behavior of the CDs was investigated by collecting the emission spectra at different concentrations in methanol. As shown in Fig. S4, the emission peak position of the CDs is slightly red-shifted from 607 to 614 nm with the concentration of the CDs increasing from 0.025 to 0.3 mg∙mL−1, which may be induced by increasing dot-dot interaction, morphology change and aggregation highly associate with concentration [28,29]. But the emission intensity of the CDs arrives at the highest at the concentration of 0.05 mg∙mL−1, and then decreases with the concentration increasing, resulting from aggregation induced quenching. In addition, high optical density or turbidity at a higher concentration may also lead to reduction in fluorescence intensity in CDs [30].

Shown in Fig. 3C and D are the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the CDs dispersed in aprotic and protic solvents, respectively. Positive solvatochromism of the CDs is revealed since the absorption band shifts to long wavelength with an increase in polarity in aprotic solvents (Fig. 3C). However, the phenomenon is not obvious in the protic solvents due to the effect of H bonds.

Excitation independence and solvatochromism are the characteristics of molecular state in CDs [31,32]. Therefore, it is inferred that the emission of the CDs in this work is controlled by the molecular state. In aprotic solvents, the dipole–dipole interactions caused the change in the electron density distribution, and narrowed the energy gap, thereof, resulting in red shifts for the absorption and fluorescence spectra [33]. An earlier study reported that solvatochromism of carbon nanomaterial in aprotic solvents is due to the morphological change [34]. In this work, the size of the CDs is enlarged with an increase in the polarity of aprotic solvent. Therefore, the solvatochromic effect of the CDs in polar aprotic solvent may originate from the combination of the dipole–dipole interactions, and the morphology and size change. In protic solvents, hydrogen bonds formed between the molecular chromophore in the CDs and the protic solvents could decrease the collision between the dots and conformational change, therefore, weakened the solvatochromism effect from the polarity of the solvents [35]. In order to confirm the solvatochromism behavior of the CDs, their FT-IR spectra in the corresponding solvents were collected. From Fig S5 and S6, the characteristic absorption peaks of molecular chromophore on the CDs are shifted or the absorbance is weakened in different solvents, suggesting that the solvatochromism of the CDs resulted from the change of molecular chromophore induced by the polarity of the solvents and H bonds.

A comparison of the CDs with solvatochromism effect is performed in Table S2. Most of the CDs with solvatochromism effect were originated from o-, m-, p- phenylenediamine isomers. And their wavelength range of solvatochromism is commonly located in green to red. Their PLQY in organic solvents are very high, and generally larger than 30%. A few CDs with solvatochromism effect were originated from citric acid, β-resorcylic acid, and p-BQ and L-Cystein. But their wavelength range of solvatochromism is distributed in blue to green region with high PLQY in organic solvents

Since the CDs are precipitated in neutral aqueous solution and the fluorescence emission is quenched due to aggregation induced quenching (AIQ) effect, a detection method of water in the organic solvents could be developed by using the CDs as a probe.

Firstly, the effect of pH on the fluorescence property of the CDs was investigated by measuring the emission intensity at 607 nm under excitation at 500 nm in phosphate buffer solutions at different pH values. As shown in Fig. S7A, the fluorescence of the CDs is stronger under neutral and weakly acidic conditions. Also, the effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence of the CDs was evaluated in the solution of NaCl at various concentrations, and no significant change in intensity of the CDs is found as the concentration of NaCl varies (Fig. S7B), suggesting that detection using the CDs as a probe will not be influenced by the change of ionic strength induced by the concentration variation of analytes. The effects of metal ions on the fluorescence of the CDs were evaluated by collecting the emission spectra in the presence of different metal ions. The CDs shows quenching response to Al3+, Cu2+ and Mn2+, while the other tested metal ions exert negligible effects on the fluorescence of the CDs (see Fig. S7C). However, trace Al3+, Cu2+ and Mn2+ in a small amount of water contained in organic solvents will not exert a significant effect on the detection using the CDs as a probe.

As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs in methanol is gradually quenched with an increase in the content of water, resulting from the AIQ effect confirmed by Fig. S8. In the range from 2.0% to 20.0%, an excellent linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the content of water in methanol was found with a calibration equation of F = 3004.88–56.89 W (R2 = 0.993, see Fig. 4B), and the limit of detection was estimated to be 0.46%. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 4C, the emission of the CDs in acetone is red shifted upon addition of H2O. And with an increase in water content, the average particle size of the CDs in acetone slightly decreases (see Fig. S9). It is reasoned that the solvent polarity of acetone was increased as the content of H2O was enhanced. Very interestingly, there is a linear relationship between the emission wavelength of CDs and the content of H2O in the range from 0.1% to 2.50% (see Fig. 4D), with a calibration equation of E = 578.69 + 6.46 W (R2 = 0.995), the limit of detection was estimated to be 0.07%.

Acetone and methanol spiked with different contents of H2O were applied as real samples to evaluate the feasibility of the as proposed methods for the determination of H2O in organic solvents. As presented in Table 1 , the recoveries are in the range of 98.2% to 101.7% for the detection of H2O in acetone, and 98.1% to 100.8% for the detection of H2O in methanol. The spiked H2O in acetone and methanol can be recovered with accuracy, which indicates that the as-proposed method has a high potential for the determination of H2O in organic solvents.

In order to further investigate the solvatochromism of the CDs in the binary mixture of liquid i.e., water-methanol and water–acetone, the preferential solvation factor (η) of the CDs in the mixture solution was calculated at different ratios of organic solvents to water. As shown in Table S3, η < 1 for both the binary mixtures of water-methanol and water–acetone at different ratios indicates that the CDs had been preferentially solvated by the less polar component (methanol and acetone) of the binary mixtures [36]. With the content of water increasing, η became larger, suggesting that the addition of water decrease the solvation of the CDs by the organic solvent, resulting in positive solvatochromism.

In conclusion, the CDs synthesized by one-step hydrothermal heating treatment of o-phenylenediamine in H2SO4 solution can be well dispersed in organic solvents but precipitated in neutralized aqueous solution. Excitation independence in organic solvents and solvatochromism induced by the polarity of solvents suggest that the emission of the CDs is controlled by molecular state. According to AIQ effect and solvatochromism induced by an increase in the solvent polarity with the content of H2O increasing, detection methods for water in organic solvents can be developed by using the CDs as a probe. The spiked H2O in acetone and methanol can be recovered with accuracy, which indicates that the as-proposed method has a high potential for the determination of H2O in organic solvents.

Xiaoyu Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Xiuming Teng: Data curation, Software. Xiaobo Sun: Resources. Wei Pan: Supervision, Resources. Jinping Wang: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Key R&D Program of China (2017YFE0105200), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31572181) and Key Research and Development Project of Shandong Province (No. 2018GNC110006).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120547.

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
